g.data-narration: Welcome! Today we'll learn how to return objects from functions in C plus plus. This is a key concept for university exams and real-world programming.
g.text: university 2022 Scheme · CSE · Complete Visual Guide

g.data-narration: When functions create or modify objects, they must pass them back to the caller. Choosing the wrong return method causes bugs, memory leaks, or slow code.
C plus plus offers three primary methods to return objects: by value, by reference, and by pointer. Each has unique trade-offs in safety and speed.
Return by value creates an independent copy. The function builds an object locally, then the copy constructor creates a new object for the caller. Very safe!
g.text: 📦 Return by Value — How It Works

g.data-narration: Here's a concrete example. The createRectangle function builds a local Rectangle and returns it by value. The caller gets a fresh, independent copy.
When returning by value, the copy constructor creates the returned copy. In C plus plus eleven and later, the move constructor can transfer ownership more efficiently.
Compilers can optimize away copies entirely using Return Value Optimization. The object is constructed directly in the caller's memory. No copy or move needed!
g.text: string getMessage() { return "Hello"; } // Constructed directly in caller's memory

g.data-narration: Return by reference avoids copying by returning an alias. The key rule: the referenced object must outlive the function call. Never return reference to a local variable!
g.text: 🔗 Return by Reference — Alias, Not Copy

g.data-narration: Method chaining uses return by reference beautifully. Each method returns star-this, allowing sequential calls on the same object. Like cout uses the insertion operator.
g.text: Each call returns same object → enables chaining!
Also used: cout << a << b << endl;

g.data-narration: Critical warning! Never return a reference to a local variable. When the function ends, the local is destroyed, leaving a dangling reference. This causes undefined behavior.
g.text: ⚠️ DANGER: Dangling Reference — Never Do This!
🔴 Undefined Behavior — university Exam Favorite Question!

g.data-narration: Return by pointer uses dynamic memory allocation with new. The caller receives a memory address and must manage the object's lifetime. Essential for factory patterns.
The factory pattern uses return by pointer for polymorphism. A base class pointer can point to any derived object, decided at runtime. Essential for design patterns.
Returning a const reference provides efficient read-only access to internal data. No copy is made, and the caller cannot modify the original. Perfect for getter functions.
g.text: 🔒 Returning const Reference — Safe Read-Only Access

g.data-narration: Here's your exam cheat sheet! Compare all three methods side by side. Remember: value is safest, reference is fastest for existing objects, pointer enables polymorphism.
g.text: 📊 Comparison — Safety vs Performance vs Control

g.data-narration: To summarize: use value for safety, reference for chaining and existing objects, pointer for polymorphism. Avoid dangling references. Prefer smart pointers in modern C plus plus.
g.text: 1️⃣ By Value = safest, use for local objects
4️⃣ NEVER return ref to local variable!
6️⃣ Use smart pointers in modern C++
